title: Cedarwood and orange beeswax pillar candle
subtitle: A poured beeswax pillar candle with cedarwood and sweet orange essential oils
excerpt: Beeswax burns longer and brighter than soy or paraffin wax of the same weight. Its natural honey note is a warm background to the cedarwood-orange fragrance blend. Beeswax pillar candles are poured into a mould rather than a container, which means unmoulding rather than setting in the jar.
sourceNotes: Beeswax pillar candle technique from The Candle Maker's Store guide (thecandlemakersstore.co.uk, May 2026). Beeswax fragrance load rates from supplier datasheet. Cedarwood-orange blending ratios from Gracefruit fragrance-making guide (gracefruit.com).

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 pillar candle (approximately 250 g, 8 cm tall, 7 cm diameter)
recipe.batchNotes: Pillar candle moulds are usually single-unit. Run multiple moulds in parallel on the same pour day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure beeswax pillar candles for at least 1 week before burning. The fragrance integrates and the wax hardens fully during the cure period.
recipe.temperatureNote: Add fragrance at 70 degrees Celsius. Pour at 70 to 75 degrees Celsius. Beeswax has a higher melt point than soy wax and must be poured hotter to fill the mould before it sets.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 5 years or more. Beeswax does not go rancid. A white bloom (beeswax bloom) may develop over time: buff away with a soft cloth. The sweet orange note fades faster than the cedarwood; after 2 years the candle will be predominantly woody.

recipeTools.notes: A rigid pillar mould (aluminium or acrylic, 7 cm diameter)

glossaryTerms.definition: A white or pale grey powdery coating that appears on natural beeswax over time as softer wax components migrate to the surface. Harmless. Buff with a soft cloth to restore the original finish.

A free-standing candle poured into a mould and unmoulded after setting, as opposed to a container candle that stays in the vessel it was poured into. Pillar candles need a harder wax: beeswax, paraffin, or pillar-grade soy wax. Container-grade soy wax is too soft for a pillar.

content.text: Beeswax is naturally harder than soy or paraffin at the same temperature, which makes it suitable for a

that must stand on its own. Beeswax also holds fragrance at lower percentages than soy wax; 3% essential oil by weight gives a noticeable scent without the wax resisting the addition. Use a mould release spray or a thin film of jojoba oil on the mould interior before pouring to aid unmoulding.

Beeswax contracts as it cools and typically sinks in the centre of a pillar candle. Plan for a second top-up pour. After the first pour has set and the centre has sunk, re-melt a small quantity of wax at the same temperature and pour carefully into the sinkhole to level the top. One top-up pour is usually sufficient.

items.prepNote: 2% of wax weight; the base note
A square-braid or flat cotton wick sized for 7 cm diameter. Thread through the mould before pouring.

content.text: Lightly oil the inside of the pillar mould with jojoba oil or a thin coat of mould release spray. Thread the wick through the mould base, pull taut, and tie the free end around a rod or pencil resting across the top of the mould so the wick is centred and under tension.

Melt the beeswax pellets in the pouring pitcher over a simmering-water bath. Beeswax has a higher melt point than soy wax: it will be fully liquid at around 65 to 70 degrees Celsius. Allow to cool to 70 degrees Celsius, stir in both essential oils, and pour into the prepared mould. Leave undisturbed in a cool place for 4 to 6 hours. When the top has sunk, re-melt a small amount of wax and top up to level. Leave to set completely for a further 2 hours before unmoulding.

To unmould, place the mould in the freezer for 10 minutes. The wax will contract slightly further and slide out cleanly. If it sticks, warm the outside of the mould briefly with a hairdryer. Trim the wick to 5 mm. Cure for at least 1 week before the first burn.
5 years or more in a cool, dark place. A white surface
is normal and harmless. Buff with a soft cloth to restore the original surface finish.
